content:
# Romans Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_006_page_001.jpg

### Freedom from Sin's Power

1. Well then, should we keep on sinning so that God can show us more and more of his wonderful grace?

2. Of course not! Since we have died to sin, how can we continue to live in it?

3. Or have you forgotten that when we were joined with Christ Jesus in baptism, we joined him in his death?

4. For we died and were buried with Christ by baptism. And just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glorious power of the Father, now we also may live new lives.

5. Since we have been united with him in his death, we will also be raised to life as he was.

6. We know that our old sinful selves were crucified with Christ so that sin might lose its power in our lives. We are no longer slaves to sin.

7. For when we died with Christ we were set free from the power of sin.

8. And since we died with Christ, we know we will also live with him.

9. We are sure of this because Christ was raised from the dead, and he will never die again. Death no longer has any power over him.

10. When he died, he died once to break the power of sin. But now that he lives, he lives for the glory of God.

11. So you also should consider yourselves to be dead to the power of sin and alive to God through Christ Jesus.

12. Do not let sin control the way you live; do not give in to sinful desires.

13. Do not let any part of your body become an instrument of evil to serve sin. Instead, give yourselves completely to God, for you were dead, but now you have new life. So use your whole body as an instrument to do what is right for the glory of God.

14. Sin is no longer your master, for you no longer live under the requirements of the law. Instead, you live under the freedom of God's grace.

### Freedom to Obey God

15. Well then, since God's grace has set us free from the law, does that mean we can go on sinning? Of course not!

16. Don't you realize that you become the slave of whatever you choose to obey? You can be a slave to sin, which leads to death, or you can choose to obey God, which leads to righteous living.

17. Thank God! Once you were slaves of sin, but now you wholeheartedly obey this teaching we have given you.

18. Now you are free from your slavery to sin, and you have become slaves to righteous living.

19. Because of the weakness of your human nature, I am using the illustration of slavery to help you understand all this. Previously, you let yourselves be slaves to impurity and lawlessness, which led ever deeper into sin. Now you must give yourselves to be slaves to righteous living so that you will become holy.

20. When you were slaves to sin, you were free from the obligation to do right.

21. And what was the result? You are now ashamed of the things you used to do, things that end in eternal doom.

22. But now you are free from the power of sin and have become slaves of God. Now you do those things that lead to holiness and result in eternal life.

23. For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life through Christ Jesus our Lord.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — The obvious objection:** If grace increases where sin increases (5:20), then why not sin more? Paul knew this question was coming. In fact, if your theology of grace never provokes this question, your grace probably isn't big enough. A grace that can't be abused isn't grace — it's law in disguise. Paul's answer is not to limit grace but to explain what grace does: it kills the old self and raises a new one.

**Verses 3-4 — Baptism as death and resurrection:** Paul doesn't see baptism as a religious ceremony. He sees it as a funeral — your funeral. You go into the water as the old you. You come out as someone new. The old self — the one enslaved to sin, the one stuck in Adam's story — is dead. Drowned. Buried. And something new has come up out of the water: a person alive to God, walking in resurrection life. Baptism is not a badge of belonging; it's a death certificate.

**Verse 14 — "Not under law but under grace":** This is the transition from the old regime to the new. Under law, you know what you should do but can't do it. Under grace, you've been empowered to become who you already are in Christ. The difference is not between rules and no rules — it's between command without power and identity with power.

**Verse 23 — The gospel in an equation:** Death is a wage — you earn it. Eternal life is a gift — you receive it. Paul uses ὀψώνια, a military term for a soldier's pay packet. Sin is the employer, and its payroll is death. God is not an employer; God is a giver. And the gift — χάρισμα, from the same root as χάρις (grace) — is life. You don't earn life. You don't deserve life. You receive it. That's the gospel.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
